title: SEGMENT 3 — Cloning GFP into other organisms: Chalfie's worm

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Cloning GFP into other organisms: Chalfie's worm
The person who took Prasher's cloned gene and made the field is Martin Chalfie at Columbia University.

Chalfie worked on the nematode worm Caenorhabditis elegans — a one-millimeter-long, transparent roundworm with exactly 959 cells that has been one of the great workhorse animals of biology for half a century.
Chalfie heard about GFP at a seminar in 1989, and the idea hit him immediately.
He was trying to study which genes were turned on in which cells of the worm.
The methods at the time were painful, slow, and indirect.

But what if, he thought, you could fuse the GFP gene to the regulatory region of any worm gene — to the bit of DNA that controls when and where the gene gets turned on — and put that construct into the worm?
Then the cells that normally turned on that gene would just glow.
You'd see expression patterns with your eyes, in a living animal, in real time.
The experiment had one critical question hanging over it: would GFP actually fold and glow inside a worm?
Or inside a bacterium?
Or inside a human cell?
Because here's the thing.
Lots of proteins, when you take their gene out of one organism and try to express it in another, fail.
They misfold.
They aggregate.
They sit there inert.

The worry, very reasonable in 1989, was that GFP might need jellyfish-specific helper enzymes to mature properly — some piece of jellyfish-only machinery that finishes the protein and turns on its glow.
If that were true, the gene by itself would be useless in any other organism.
Chalfie's lab, working with Prasher's cloned gene, put it into E. coli first — the standard bacterium of every biology lab on Earth.
And the bacteria glowed green.
Then they put it into C. elegans, into the worm, under the control of a regulatory region that turns on in specific sensory neurons.
And the worm's sensory neurons lit up like jewels inside a transparent body.
A paper went out in Science in 1994 with those pictures, and that paper, more than any other, launched the fluorescent protein era.
The world-changing point Chalfie established wasn't really "you can put GFP in a worm.
" It was the deeper point that GFP, once it's been translated by a ribosome, can finish making itself glow without any outside help at all.
No jellyfish enzymes required.
No special cofactors.
The 238 amino acids of GFP contained, within themselves, every instruction the protein needed to fold up and turn into a glowing molecule.
Plant cells, yeast cells, fly cells, mouse cells, human cells — it didn't matter.
If the cell could read DNA and make a protein, GFP would glow inside it.
Think about what that means practically.
Hand someone the GFP gene, and they can make any cell — in any organism on Earth, basically — glow green.
They can mark which cells are expressing a particular gene.
They can tag any protein they like and watch it move.
They can light up specific neurons, specific organelles, specific tissues.
The cell does all the work.
You just provide the recipe.
This is also why the molecule traveled so fast.
Within a few years of Chalfie's paper, GFP was in thousands of labs.
Plant biologists were lighting up cell walls.
Yeast geneticists were tracking the cell cycle.
Neuroscientists were marking individual neurons.
Developmental biologists were watching cell lineages unfold in transparent embryos.
The whole field had been waiting, without knowing it, for exactly this tool.
But Chalfie's GFP — the wild-type protein, straight from the jellyfish — had limitations.
It was dim.
It folded a little slowly at body temperature.
It had an awkward dual absorption spectrum.
And it came in exactly one color: green.
If you wanted to label two proteins at the same time, in the same cell, with different colors so you could tell them apart — you couldn't, because there was only green.
That brings us to the third figure in this Nobel-winning trio.
Roger Tsien, at the University of California, San Diego.
Tsien was a chemist of extraordinary range.
Before GFP, he had basically single-handedly invented the field of small-molecule calcium indicators — chemical dyes you could load into a cell to measure calcium concentrations.
When GFP came along, he turned his entire lab onto it.
Tsien was not satisfied with what nature had handed him.
He wanted to engineer GFP from the ground up — to make it brighter, faster, sharper, and available in any color of the visible spectrum you might want.
Tsien's lab became, essentially, the workshop where the rainbow got built.

He and his colleagues spent the next fifteen years systematically mutating GFP, screening the variants for better behavior, and discovering — and inventing — variants in blue, cyan, green, yellow, and eventually, with help from a coral protein, in orange and red.
By the early 2000s, you could pick almost any color along the visible spectrum and find a fluorescent protein in that color, engineered to be bright and well-behaved.

That palette is what made fluorescent proteins genuinely useful as a tool for serious cell biology, because most interesting biology requires tracking more than one thing at a time, and to do that you need different colors.
In 2008, the Nobel Prize in Chemistry was shared by three people.
Shimomura — for the discovery.
Chalfie — for the demonstration that you could express GFP in other organisms.
And Tsien — for the engineering of the variants.
Three legs of a relay, each indispensable.
Prasher, who cloned the gene, was not included; the Nobel rules permit only three winners.
The omission has been widely noted.
Prasher attended the ceremony, by Chalfie and Tsien's invitation.
That's a small but human detail worth remembering.
So that's the history.

Now let's open up the molecule itself and see how it works — because the structure of GFP is one of the most elegant solutions to a biochemical problem in all of nature, and once you see it, every variant we discuss later makes sense.
